EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved north into southern New England late on the 22nd and early on the 23rd.  There was elevated instability and a considerable amount of effective shear (around 40 kt) in the elevated-convective level, due to strong mid-upper level SSW flow. One cluster of thunderstorms produced quarter-size hail when it came onshore in Westerly, RI.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported on social media that hail up to quarter size occurred in Westerly, along with frequent lightning.  Report was relayed by amateur radio.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent mid level shortwave moved in from the Great Lakes bringing widespread wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Missing 15 year old boy due to rip currents. A boat was requested to assist lifeguards doing the search. The search was eventually suspended.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deamplifying mid level shortwave with dewpoints in the 70s and decent instability brought scattered thunderstorms and widespread flash flooding to Rhode Island and Massachusetts. There were many streets closed, many cars stranded in flood waters, and several houses with flooded basements or first floors.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Pawtucket, cars were stuck in flood waters near Armisticle Blvd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and muggy airmass was conducive to heavy downpours over eastern Massachusetts and Rhode Island when a de-amplifying shortwave moved across the region. It also had support from a moderately strong jet aloft and plenty of moisture. Weak boundary layer winds made for slow moving storms which contributed to flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Providence, multiple water rescues were done for vehicles stranded in flood waters. Rt 10 was closed and several manhole covers were pushed up.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system brought a period of heavy rain, flash flooding, and embedded thunderstorms to southern New England from the morning to mid evening. In addition to much tree damage from straight line winds, there was one tornado in Connecticut, 2 in Rhode Island, and 1 in Massachusetts. These all occurred in the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Woonsocket, a car was stuck in flooding on Railroad St.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 with maximum winds of 100 mph began in a wooded area|between Chopmist Hill Road (Route 102) and Bungy Road in the town|of Glocester, RI here the tornado either snapped or uprooted an|estimated 75 or more, healthy and mature trees. The tornado moved|northeast and crossed Bungy Road before dissipating over an open|field. Most notable, a small outbuilding, used as bus stop shelter|was blown away by the tornado with remnants littered west of the|original location. Some pieces were found an a neighbors roof.|This damage and the tree damage along the path was consistent |with wind speeds between 90 and 100 mph, resulting an EF-1 rating |on the Enhanced Fujita Scale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep longwave trough, south-southwest flow at all levels, and the development of a QLCS (quasi-linear convective system) created all the ingredients for a widespread and susbstantial flash flood event across much of southern New England.  For a few hours during the late morning, helicity values were high and a brief tornado formed along the QLCS in North Brookfield, MA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Cranston, Oaklawn Ave was flooded and impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southerly low-level jet ahead of an approaching cold front moved across southeastern New England shortly after midnight on January 26th.  A low pressure system tracked northeast along the front from roughly Philadelphia to Boston during the overnight hours, producing some light snow inland but 1 to 2.5 inches of rain and strong/high wind in Rhode Island, southeast Massachusetts, and the adjacent coastal waters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 108 AM EST, a mesonet station at New Shoreham recorded a wind gust to 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent mid level low/shortwave deamplified as it moved east-southeast from the Ohio Valley late on the 27th and early on the 28th.  At the surface, while the parent low lifted across the Great Lakes, a secondary low developed over DelMarVa in response to height falls from approaching trough...then moved east, passing well south of southern New England.  The result was a northwest-to-southeast oriented band of heavy snow from western MA to northern and eastern CT to southern RI.  Six to nine inches of snow fell in those regions.  There was a very sharp cutoff to the northeast, with only one to two inches falling in northeastern MA and Cape Cod.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall in northwest Providence County generally ranged from 5.5 to 7.0 inches, with the highest amounts in the higher terrain.  Some specific amounts included: 7.0 inches in Foster; 6.3 inches in Glocester; 6.0 inches in Burrillville; and 5.5 inches in Smithfield.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall generally ranged from 6 to 8 inches in western Kent County.  Some specific amounts included 8.3 inches in West Greenwich and 6.3 inches in West Warwick.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent mid level shortwave moved in from the Great Lakes bringing widespread wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fifteen year old Jaimer Ico Gregorio from New London, Connecticut, was with his family at Dunes Park Beach in Westerly, Rhode Island, Thursday night when he went under the water and never resurfaced. There were no lifeguards on duty at the time. A search was launched on the evening of the 27th but his body was not recovered until the evening of Sunday the 30th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major Arctic cold front blasted through southern New England on the morning of March 3rd. It was followed by a few rounds of strong and locally damaging wind gusts. Temperatures plunged to 10 to 20 below zero on the morning of March 4th. The -10F reading at Boston's Logan Airport was the coldest there since 1957.  There were numerous instances of burst pipes due to the cold across the region, but it is impossible to quantify dollar damage.  Wind chill indices reached 30 to 40 below zero across southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1242 PM EST, a Weatherflow mesonet station at Block Island Jetty recorded a wind gust to 60 mph.

Wind chill indices reached 30 to 40 below zero across southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds generally were gusting to 45 to 55 mph.  In Foster at 530 PM EST, a tree was down on wires, causing a utility pole to break on Pine Tree Lane and Winsor Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high amplitude, negatively tilted trough moving across the Great Lakes and into New England along with cold and warm front provided strong forcing which resulted in flooding, wind damage, and 5 tornadoes. In the warm sector dewpoints surged into the mid 70s with PWATs near 2 inches which led to torrential downpours. Tornadoes occurred in Scotland, Ct, Scituate, RI, North Attleboro, MA, Stoughton, MA, and Weymouth, MA. The tornadoes ranged in intensity from EF-0 to EF-2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In East Greenwich, Rt 2 at Middle St was flooded and impassable.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Scituate, there were numerous trees down on Byron Randall Rd. Additionally, a tree was down on a home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalous closed low over southern New England caused numerous showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening.  One storm caused lightning damage in Rhode Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Hopkinton, a house on Highview Avenue was struck by lightning, reportedly causing a small fire, which was quickly extinguished.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Near-record heat and a strong short-wave trough digging southeastward across Quebec led to the development of afternoon thunderstorms, mainly along the higher terrain in central MA and northeast CT. A few of these storms produced isolated wind damage and some large hail.  In Rhode Island there was one report of sub-severe sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Foster, there were several reports of dime size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave trough moved across southern New England overnight, triggering a few severe thunderstorms in a very warm and moist environment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Richmond, a tree and wires were down at the intersection of Kenyon Hill Trail and Switch Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave trough moved across southern New England overnight, triggering a few severe thunderstorms in a very warm and moist environment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Foster, a tree was down on the 100 block of Danielson Pike.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave trough moved across southern New England overnight, triggering a few severe thunderstorms in a very warm and moist environment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Portsmouth, a house was hit by lightning on Slocum Road, causing a basement fire. Report relayed by amateur radio.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave trough moved across southern New England overnight, triggering a few severe thunderstorms in a very warm and moist environment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Glocester, a tree was down on the 300 block of Cooper Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave trough moved across southern New England overnight, triggering a few severe thunderstorms in a very warm and moist environment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Burrillville, a large tree was down on Buck Hill Road near the fire tower.  Report relayed by amateur radio.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved up the Atlantic coast Sunday night into Monday, merging with a short wave trough exiting the northern Great Plains and taking the surface low west of southern New England. This brought strong southerly winds and coastal flooding to southern New England. This was a very impactful storm flooding roads, closing schools, knocking out power to 300,000 across MA, cancelling flights, and killing one man in southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Westerly at 10:14 AM EST there was coastal flooding on Atlantic Ave at Winnapaug Rd which lasted until 11:30 AM EST. In Narragansett at 10:45 AM EST Narragansett Ave at Boston Neck Rd, Beach Rd by the town beach, and Ocean Ave were closed. Later, at 11:10 AM EST there was coastal flooding at Galilee and Ocean Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved up the Atlantic coast Sunday night into Monday, merging with a short wave trough exiting the northern Great Plains and taking the surface low west of southern New England. This brought strong southerly winds and coastal flooding to southern New England. This was a very impactful storm flooding roads, closing schools, knocking out power to 300,000 across MA, cancelling flights, and killing one man in southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Newport at 11:10 AM EST Ocean Ave in the Brenton Point area and Memorial Blvd from Eustis Ave to the Middletown line were closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid level trough and a quasi-stationary boundary across Southern New England led to widespread flash flooding in the late hours of the 3rd into the 4th. Most of the flooding was in Rhode Island and Connecticut where cars became stuck in water, and several basements were flooded. In MA a few roads were closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Cranston, Pleasant St was flooded and impassable.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Providence, the intersection of Francis St and West Exchange St closed due to flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Providence, Jastram St was flooded with 5 cars stuck in floodwaters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid level trough and a quasi-stationary boundary across Southern New England led to widespread flash flooding in the late hours of the 3rd into the 4th. Most of the flooding was in Rhode Island and Connecticut where cars became stuck in water, and several basements were flooded. In MA a few roads were closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Pawtucket, an entire basement level of an apartment complex was compromised due to flooding. American Red Cross requested to help displaced residents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deamplifying mid level shortwave with dewpoints in the 70s and decent instability brought scattered thunderstorms and widespread flash flooding to Rhode Island and Massachusetts. There were many streets closed, many cars stranded in flood waters, and several houses with flooded basements or first floors.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Lincoln, Breakneck Hill Rd was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system brought a period of heavy rain, flash flooding, and embedded thunderstorms to southern New England from the morning to mid evening. In addition to much tree damage from straight line winds, there was one tornado in Connecticut, 2 in Rhode Island, and 1 in Massachusetts. These all occurred in the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 with maximum winds of 100 mph began east of North Central|State Airport along Wellington Road in the town of Lincoln, RI. A|video did show a funnel cloud passing over the adjacent airport|but found no damage. The tornado uprooted a large tree and damaged|am estimated 20' by 30' section of roof on a building across the|street and due north of the uprooted tree. Video provided by|Lincoln Animal Control showed a thin tornado passing at the end of|the facility's driveway and twisting a large branch from a tree.|The tornado moved northeast into a field of solar panels which|uplifted two sections. From here, the tornado likely lifted before|moving across Route 146 at Route 116 which was documented on a DOT|camera. This damage and the tree damage along the path was |consistent with wind speeds between 90 and 100 mph, resulting an |EF-1 rating on the Enhanced Fujita Scale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved up the Atlantic coast Sunday night into Monday, merging with a short wave trough exiting the northern Great Plains and taking the surface low west of southern New England. This brought strong southerly winds and coastal flooding to southern New England. This was a very impactful storm flooding roads, closing schools, knocking out power to 300,000 across MA, cancelling flights, and killing one man in southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Providence at 12:35 PM EST a car was stuck in coastal flooding in a park area on River Rd next to Narragansett Bay.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds generally gusted 50 to 65 mph. The highest gust was 65 mph at Fox Point in Providence. In East Providence at 9:20 AM EST a brick facade was ripped off of the Healthtrax building on Catamore Blvd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously amplified mid level trough moving east from the Great Lakes closed off and brought widespread showers and scattered thunderstorms which lead to flooding in Rhode Island.  Many roads were closed and cars were stuck in flood waters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Providence, Rt 146 had significant flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system brought a period of heavy rain, flash flooding, and embedded thunderstorms to southern New England from the morning to mid evening. In addition to much tree damage from straight line winds, there was one tornado in Connecticut, 2 in Rhode Island, and 1 in Massachusetts. These all occurred in the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Woonsocket, two cars were stuck in street flooding on Mandeville Hill Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deamplifying mid level shortwave with dewpoints in the 70s and decent instability brought scattered thunderstorms and widespread flash flooding to Rhode Island and Massachusetts. There were many streets closed, many cars stranded in flood waters, and several houses with flooded basements or first floors.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In North Providence, Branch Ave was closed with cars stranded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high amplitude, negatively tilted trough moving across the Great Lakes and into New England along with cold and warm front provided strong forcing which resulted in flooding, wind damage, and 5 tornadoes. In the warm sector dewpoints surged into the mid 70s with PWATs near 2 inches which led to torrential downpours. Tornadoes occurred in Scotland, Ct, Scituate, RI, North Attleboro, MA, Stoughton, MA, and Weymouth, MA. The tornadoes ranged in intensity from EF-0 to EF-2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado caused significant damage along a discontinuous path|in Scituate, Johnston, and North Providence, Rhode Island. This |is the strongest tornado to have struck Rhode Island since the F-2|tornado in Cranston and Providence on August 7, 1986.||The tornado first touched down near Byron Randall Road in Scituate|which is where the most severe damage occurred. There were hundreds|of large trees either uprooted or snapped at their bases. One |home sustained damage to its roof, the top of its chimney was |blown off, windows were blown in, and an exterior door was |dislodged from its framing. Damage was consistent with winds of |around 115 mph which is classified as EF-2 on the Enhanced Fujita|Scale.||The tornado then tracked into Johnston where it crossed I-295 at |Exit 10 and lifted a vehicle into the air before dropping it back |onto the highway. The driver was transported to an area hospital |with minor injuries. From there, the tornado moved across Bridle |Way and Carriage Way where a number of trees were snapped or |uprooted, some of which fell onto homes or vehicles. Some homes |also lost some singles from their roofs. A metal Stop sign pole |was bent in half and the sign was blown away. The tornado then |caused damage in Highland Memorial Park Cemetery where a number of|large trees were either snapped or uprooted. The damage observed |in Johnston was consistent with winds of 90 to 100 mph which is |classified as EF-1 on the Enhanced Fujita Scale.||Finally, the tornado crossed into North Providence. Similar to|Johnston, a number of trees were either snapped or uprooted, some|falling onto homes or vehicles. Most of the damage observed was to|the north of Mineral Spring Avenue. One of the harder hit areas  |included Lydia Avenue, Armand Drive, and Bennett Street where two|homes were made uninhabitable from fallen trees. The damage |observed in North Providence was consistent with winds of 90 to |100 mph which is classified as EF-1 on the Enhanced Fujita Scale.||The National Weather Service would like to thank the Rhode Island|Emergency Management Agency, the Scituate Police Department, the|Johnston Police Department, the North Providence Fire Department,|and Skywarn Amateur Radio Operators for their assistance with the|damage survey.
